reason: The critical omission of step ① causes the code example to reference `systemAccount` and `users` as undeclared variables, making it non-compilable. An AI agent presenting this code to a developer would produce broken, misleading guidance. Phase E returned the file unchanged — no fixes were applied to any of the three findings.

findings_assessment.justification: The code uses `systemAccount.getLoginId()`, `users.getKanjiName()`, etc. but neither variable is declared anywhere in the example. `W11AC02Form`, `SystemAccountEntity`, and `UsersEntity` are also absent. The result is non-compilable code that omits the mandatory first step of the documented 4-step process. A developer following this example would get a compile error with no indication of what is missing. Why it persisted: Phase E output is byte-for-byte identical to the Phase D input — the fix agent did not modify the file at all.

The `ctx.setRequestScopedVar("11AC_W11AC01", successionForm)` block is the only place in this document that shows how to pass state to the next action via request scope. Omitting it means a developer implementing this flow has no guidance on inter-action data handoff, a pattern specific to this Nablarch idiom. Why it persisted: same as above — Phase E made no changes to the file.

`ApplicationException` appears in the code block itself, so a developer who has already retrieved s2 will see it. The missing hint only reduces discoverability when the user queries specifically for `ApplicationException` — a narrower failure mode. Why it persisted: same as above — Phase E made no changes to the file.
